elements.sent-2411-001: Training multimodal generative models on large, uncurated datasets can result in users being exposed to harmful, unsafe and controversial or culturally-inappropriate outputs.
elements.sent-2411-002: While model editing has been proposed to remove or filter undesirable concepts in embedding and latent spaces, it can inadvertently damage learned manifolds, distorting concepts in close semantic proximity.
elements.sent-2411-003: We identify limitations in current model editing techniques, showing that even benign, proximal concepts may become misaligned.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address the need for safe content generation, we propose a modular, dynamic solution that leverages safety-context embeddings and a dual reconstruction process using tunable weighted summation in the latent space to generate safer images.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our method preserves global context without compromising the structural integrity of the learned manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-006: We achieve state-of-the-art results on safe image generation benchmarks, while offering controllable variation of model safety.
elements.sent-2411-007: We identify trade-offs between safety and censorship, which presents a necessary perspective in the development of ethical AI models.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{Figures/motivation_fig_ALT.pdf} \caption{Model editing for safe image generation often disrupts the learned manifolds, causing semantic misalignments. Even benign inputs can be misaligned in the proximity of the removed concepts (e.g. red$\rightarrow$blood/violence). In the bottom row, we show this with SafeCLIP \cite{Poppi2024}. Our editing-free approach generates safe content, preserving global visual context and manifold integrity.} \label{motivation_FIG}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/full_method_fig_expanded.pdf} \caption{(a) Typical text-to-image pipelines are susceptible to generating unsafe content. (b) We introduce an editing-free, safe text-to-image pipeline that preserves global context and learned manifolds, deploying an inappropriate content detector to identify the appropriate safety spectrum for incoming prompts/embeddings. (c) A dual latent reconstruction process, leveraging weighted latent summation, retains global context and removes unsafe content during local de-noising steps, enable effective control over safety vs. censorship.} \label{flowchart_FIG}
elements.sent-2411-009: Large generative models, particularly those designed for visual content synthesis and understanding continue to make remarkable progress \cite{Cao2024, Chen2024, Huang2024a, Peng2024, Xing2024, Xing2024_simda , learning complex semantic relationships by training on vast collections of uncurated online datasets \cite{Mehrabi2021,Cho2023_A, D'Inca2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, this data often includes harmful or explicit content \cite{Park2024, Rauh2024 , posing risks as trained models may reproduce offensive, biased, or unsafe outputs \cite{Mehrabi2021, Rauh2024, Gandikota2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: This work addresses the critical challenge of ensuring safe content generation in text-to-image models, focusing on the ethical and safety risks associated with generating harmful or explicit images.
elements.sent-2411-012: While NSFW filters \cite{Rando2022, safetyChecker are widely implemented in public-facing pipelines to block unsafe content, they remain imperfect \cite{Leu2024, Liu2024, Rando2022 , highlighting the need for more robust, multi-layered generative AI safeguards.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Model editing and concept removal have emerged as useful tools for enhancing the safety of image generation in large-scale generative models \cite{Gandikota2024, Huang2024, Poppi2024, Zhang2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, removing learned concepts disrupts semantically rich embeddings and latent spaces, causing unintended structural damage to the model's learned manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-015: Such semantic disruptions may misalign benign concepts in the close semantic proximity of the removed concept, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{motivation_FIG with examples of `red' (proximal) imagery being impacted by the removal of violent concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-016: To quantify semantic disruptions, we measure the impact of removing concepts on the surrounding regions of the edited manifold through evaluations of proximal concept embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-017: Although model editing is a practical approach for safe content generation, it necessitates careful consideration of the trade-off between maintaining model alignment and enforcing safety.
elements.sent-2411-018: In-situ model editing and concept removal methods offer effective suppression of inappropriate and unsafe content by shifting unsafe or inappropriate conditioning vectors (concepts) towards unconditioned regions of the edited model's learned manifold \cite{Gandikota2024, Huang2024, Poppi2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, completely removing guidance for unsafe concepts may hinder the model’s ability to maintain nuanced semantic relationships, highlighting the justification for auxiliary, editing-free safe content generation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-020: Hence, other approaches that deploy image in-painting \cite{Park2024 and modified guidance solutions \cite{Schramowski2023, Li2024 have also been proposed.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Ethics in AI is crucial and while fairness, reliability and trust-based AI decision support methods have been explored extensively in the literature \cite{Chiang2023, Gandikota2024, Lai2023, Ma2023, Schramowski2023, Wang2021, Yang2024 , the relationship between safety and censorship needs further examination.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Safety should not be wholly conflated with censorship unless explicitly stated by a user or model provider.
elements.sent-2411-023: Ethical concerns emerge when safety measures filter culturally relevant content, potentially causing discriminatory effects \cite{Allen2024, Glukhov2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Therefore, unlearning complex semantic relationships is not a foolproof solution.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our editing-free method (i) preserves the structural integrity of learned manifolds and (ii) offers a balanced control over safety and censorship by introducing a modular, dynamic solution.
elements.sent-2411-026: We leverage safety-context embeddings to enable safe image guidance within a dual latent reconstruction framework, employing tunable weighted summation in the latent space which offers control over safety.
elements.sent-2411-027: This approach preserves global visual context in generated images and mitigates semantic disruptions in the learned manifold, ensuring a robust and adaptable safe image generation pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-028: By modifying the traditional conditional diffusion/generation process (see Fig. \ref{flowchart_FIG ), our method integrates safety-guided AI decision support as a foundational component.}
elements.sent-2411-030: A method for quantifying semantic disruptions in learned manifolds by measuring the deformation of content generated from proximal concepts when unsafe concepts are removed.
elements.sent-2411-031: We find that removing learned concepts can adversely affect proximal concepts, shifting them up to 32\% closer to the unconditioned space.
elements.sent-2411-032: A responsible, safe text-to-image generation method that offers intuitive control over output safety and the level of censorship.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our editing-free, global context-preserving method uses dual latent reconstruction and weighted summation to generate safe images while maintaining the integrity of learned manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-034: This spectrum-based safety protocol prioritizes effective control over generated content and achieves state-of-the-art performance on the popular I2P dataset~\cite{Schramowski2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Two modular inappropriate input detectors: (a) a nearest neighbor classifier within unsafe-labeled clusters in the embedding space and, (b) an integrated LLM that identifies harmful content based on safety protocol labels.
elements.sent-2411-036: Each module effectively guides unsafe inputs toward safer semantic regions.
elements.sent-2411-037: We achieve up to 100\% binary (inappropriate or not) detection accuracy and 78\% class prediction accuracy on I2P~\cite{Schramowski2023 and ViSU~\cite{Poppi2024 ~datasets.}}

elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.96\linewidth]{Figures/full_method_fig_expanded_v2.pdf} \caption{(a) Typical text-to-image pipelines are susceptible to generating unsafe content when exposed to irresponsible prompts. (b) We introduce an editing-free, safe text-to-image pipeline that preserves global context and learned manifolds. We deploy an inappropriate content detector to identify the appropriate safety spectrum for incoming embeddings, which outputs a safe embedding that facilitates our safe guidance. Unsafe and safe embeddings are used in a piecewise reconstruction setup to guide the safe image reconstruction. Combining the two latents is necessary to preserve the global visual context while still generating safe content. (c) Our dual latent reconstruction process is editing-free and preserves the global visual context of the generated image. A model provider outlines safety protocols which are used to define labeled clusters within the text-encoder embedding space. The input detector determines the unsafe class (use I2P dataset~\cite{Schramowski2023} labels) and thus, the appropriate safety spectrum. This decision informs the safe content guidance step. The irresponsible embedding is also retained in order to preserve the visual context of the generated image. Our piecewise denoising function is governed by a global context preservation threshold and similarity calculations. We deploy a combination of latents to retain global information in early denoising steps and remove unsafe content during later (local) denoising steps. Controllable hyperparameters enable effective control over the level of required safety.} \label{flowchart_FIG}
